drafts.angle: Minimal-blast-radius, incremental. The interface change is the smallest coherent one: make exactly the four driver methods (exec/run/get/all) return Promises and propagate await through @keel/db's four terminals (.get/.all/.run/.count) plus @keel/migrate. Everything that is a pure value today — defineTable, columns, conditions, renderSelect/compileInsert, createTableSql/dropTableSql, the schema builder's SQL strings — stays byte-for-byte synchronous; only the I/O boundary goes async. The SQLite dev path stays trivially async (wrap the existing sync better-sqlite3/bun:sqlite calls in `async`/Promise.resolve, no engine change). Consumers ripple in a strict dependency order chosen so the 100% coverage gate is green after EACH package's commit, never a flag-day. The Postgres adapter is additive (a new package), so it can land and be tested without touching the SQLite default. No sync-over-async shim anywhere (it would re-introduce the blocking footgun ADR 0006 exists to remove).
drafts.pgAdapterPlan:
New additive package `@keel/pg` (packages/pg/), depending on `pg` (node-postgres). It exports `openPostgres(config | poolOrConnectionString): Promise<{ db: KernelDatabase; close }>` mirroring openSqlite's shape, wrapping a `pg.Pool`. It implements the async SqlDatabase directly (no sync engine underneath, so the async interface is native, not a wrapper).

Placeholder translation: @keel/db and @keel/migrate emit SQLite-style positional `?`. The adapter's prepare(sql) rewrites `?` → `$1..$n` left-to-right at prepare time (a single pass that ignores `?` inside string literals and `::` casts — though Keel never interpolates user strings into SQL bodies, only `?`, so a plain positional counter is sufficient for the framework's own emitted SQL; document this assumption). The rewrite is memoized per prepared sql string.

Prepared-statement handling: pg has no persistent prepare() handle like better-sqlite3; instead prepare(sql) returns a lightweight statement object that closes over the translated text, and run/get/all call `pool.query({ text, values: params })` (optionally with a stable `name` for pg's server-side prepared-statement cache). get() runs the query and returns `result.rows[0] ?? undefined`; all() returns `result.rows`; run() returns `{ changes: result.rowCount, lastInsertRowid: ... }`.

lastInsertRowid gap (a real hazard): Postgres has no implicit lastInsertRowid. SQLite-style `INSERT ... ` with no RETURNING gives better-sqlite3 a rowid; @keel/db's insert.run() path returns only `{ changes }` so it is fine, but @keel/queue.enqueue relies on `result.lastInsertRowid`. The adapter must surface lastInsertRowid as `undefined`/NaN for plain inserts on PG; queue must move to a `RETURNING id` insert + `.get()` (covered in ripple). For @keel/db, `.returning().get()` already appends `RETURNING *`, which PG honors natively, so the ORM path is portable as-is.

Row mapping: pg returns snake_case column keys (matching what better-sqlite3 returns for our DDL), so @keel/db's hydrate() (byColumn snake→camel) works unchanged. Booleans: bind() already coerces JS booleans to 1/0 for SQLite; on PG a column declared BOOLEAN returns JS boolean and INTEGER 0/1 returns number — acceptable because Keel's DDL renders booleans as INTEGER 0/1 (see migrate renderDefaultClause), so PG stores/returns them as integers consistently with SQLite. Integer vs bigint: pg returns int8/COUNT(*) as strings by default; the adapter must register a type parser (or count() already does `Number(row.c)`, which coerces a numeric string fine). exec(sql) maps to `pool.query(sql)` for DDL/multi-statement (pg's simple-query protocol allows multiple statements in one exec string, matching SQLite's exec).
drafts.createAppAsyncHandling:
createApp (packages/kernel/src/kernel.ts) runs migrations on boot synchronously today via `new Migrator(config.db, ...).migrate()`. Once @keel/migrate's migrate() returns a Promise (it awaits exec + the bookkeeping prepare/all/run), createApp MUST become async: `export async function createApp(config: AppConfig): Promise<App>` and `const migrationsApplied = config.migrations === undefined ? [] : await new Migrator(config.db, config.migrations).migrate();`. The returned App.handle is already async, so only the boot path changes shape.

This is the single hard COORDINATION POINT with the router/web agent. Today createApp constructs `new Application({ router, controllers, middleware })` from @keel/web and returns an App whose handle delegates to application.handle. That agent is concurrently removing Application/Controller and changing AppConfig to a code-first keel() router. To avoid a merge collision and a flag-day:
 - This work owns ONLY: (a) the KernelDatabase interface flip, (b) making the Migrator call awaited, (c) changing createApp's RETURN TYPE to Promise<App>. 
 - This work does NOT touch AppConfig.router/controllers/middleware or the Application wiring — leave that exact block to the router agent; document that `createApp` is now `async` and every caller must `await createApp(...)` (CLI runServe, examples/blog, examples/estate, mcp test harness, integration/e2e harnesses, create-keel templates).
 - Hand the router agent a one-line contract: "createApp is now `async`; await it, and your handlers must `await` every db terminal (.get/.all/.run/.count) and `await` the now-async identity methods (verifyEmail/login/currentUser — see below)." Because their handlers are already async, the only change on their side is adding `await` — no control-flow restructuring.
 - Sequencing: land the db/migrate/kernel interface flip + the await in createApp together (kernel's own tests await createApp), but coordinate the merge so the router agent rebases onto the async createApp signature rather than the two diffs racing on the same Application block.

This is the root; nothing else compiles until it lands.

Flip types.ts SqlStatement/SqlDatabase to Promise. Migrator.ensureTable/appliedVersions/migrate/rollback/status await exec + prepare().all()/.run(). Schema builder methods (createTable/addColumn/addIndex/execute) await db.exec. The per-migration BEGIN/try/COMMIT/ROLLBACK becomes await-ed; keep each migration its own transaction. migrate() returns Promise<string[]>. Pure DDL string rendering (table-builder, renderDefaultClause) stays sync.

Wrap raw sync engine calls in async (exec/run/get/all). SqliteHandle + injected fake engines stay sync. Verifies the SQLite dev default presents the async interface; fallback branch still covered with sync fakes.

types.ts SqlStatement→Promise. sqlStore's get/set/delete/clear await the prepared statements; CacheStore interface methods become Promise-returning. Ripples into Cache facade + any in-memory store parity (keep MemoryStore async-shaped to match the CacheStore contract).

It imports SqlDatabase from @keel/migrate (already flipped). load.ts (loadEntries/loadEntry), write.ts (createEntry/updateEntry/deleteEntry/pruneEntries), persist.ts, hydrate.ts await their prepare().all()/.run(); these functions become async. pruneEntries' loop awaits each statement.run sequentially (or batch). drafts.testMatrixPlan:
Keeping 100% under the existing SERIAL Node/vitest gate (scripts/coverage-gate.ts) while proving SQLite+Postgres parity:

1. Coverage gate stays Node/vitest, per-package, serial — unchanged. The async flip does not change which branches exist; each terminal still has its hit/miss branches (e.g. get() row==null vs row), now behind `await`. Update each gated suite to `await` the terminals; coverage % is unaffected because the same branches execute.

2. SQLite is the gate's real engine: better-sqlite3 loads under Node, openSqlite's async wrapper resolves immediately, so all of @keel/db, migrate, cache, content-store, workflows, queue, identity, admin, mailing-lists keep hitting their lines exactly as today. The bun:sqlite branch in openSqlite still NEVER runs under the gate — keep testing it with the injected SqliteEngines fakes (now async-wrapped); fakes stay sync, the wrapper makes them async, both fallback arms covered.

3. Postgres parity WITHOUT breaking the gate: the new @keel/pg package's UNIT tests run under the Node gate against a FAKE async pg client (inject a Pool-shaped stub) to cover its branches (?→$n translation, rows[0]??undefined, rowCount mapping, lastInsertRowid absence, type-parser) to 100% — no live DB in the gate, matching the established "inject fake async engines" pattern so the gate has no network/container dependency and stays deterministic-serial.

4. Real-Postgres parity lives in a SEPARATE, NON-gated CI step (like @keel/integration and @keel/e2e, which the gate already skips because they declare no test:cov): a new integration suite parametrized over two adapters — openSqlite (in-memory) and openPostgres (a pg test container / service) — running the SAME journeys (identity register→verify→login, queue enqueue→claim→complete, content create→load, migrate up→rollback). This proves behavioral parity (placeholder translation, RETURNING id, snake_case hydrate, count() coercion) on a live PG without subjecting the 100%-coverage gate to container flakiness or oversubscription. The pg container only runs in this dedicated step.

5. Add an explicit parity assertion for the two known divergences: (a) lastInsertRowid — assert queue.enqueue returns a real id on BOTH engines (forces the RETURNING id migration), (b) `?`→`$n` — a translation table test plus a live round-trip with multiple params and a param reused position. Net: the gate proves coverage on SQLite+fakes; the integration step proves parity on live PG; neither weakens the other.
hazards: PARAM-SHAPE FORK: @keel/db/migrate/cache/content-store use positional `?` + unknown[]; @keel/queue uses better-sqlite3 NAMED params (@queue,@name) passed as an object. openSqlite's `...params` spread adapter only binds positional arrays. The pg `?`→$n translator also assumes positional. Queue cannot ride the positional translator as-is — either keep queue on a named-param-capable handle (no Postgres portability) or migrate queue to positional `?` + RETURNING id. This must be decided in ADR 0006 or queue's PG path silently breaks.

lastInsertRowid is SQLite-only. @keel/queue.enqueue returns Number(result.lastInsertRowid). Postgres has no implicit lastInsertRowid; the pg adapter will surface it as undefined/NaN. enqueue must switch to `INSERT ... RETURNING id`.get(). @keel/db's plain insert.run() only returns {changes} so it's safe, and .returning().get() already emits RETURNING * which PG honors.

PUBLIC INTERFACE BREAK in @keel/identity: verifyEmail/login/currentUser are SYNCHRONOUS today (return User / {user,session}). They call db helpers that become async, so they MUST become async — changing the Identity interface. The concurrently-rewritten router/web agent's handlers call login/currentUser; they must add `await`. This is a cross-agent coordination dependency, not a local change.

createApp becomes async (Promise<App>) because migrations become async. EVERY caller must await it: CLI runServe, examples/blog, examples/estate, create-keel templates, mcp/integration/e2e harnesses, and the router agent's bootstrap. A missed await yields an unhandled App promise that fails opaquely at first request.

COVERAGE GATE / TRANSACTION ATOMICITY: migrate's per-migration BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK now awaits. On PG, an exec(`BEGIN`) on a Pool is per-connection — a Pool hands out a different connection per query, so BEGIN/COMMIT issued as separate pool.query calls do NOT share a transaction. The migrator must acquire ONE dedicated connection (pool.connect()) for the BEGIN…COMMIT span, or transactions silently no-op on PG. SQLite (single connection) hides this bug; only the live-PG integration step catches it.

SQL DIALECT DRIFT beyond placeholders: queue's `UPDATE ... WHERE id = (SELECT ... LIMIT 1) RETURNING *` and `LIMIT -1 OFFSET n` (renderSelect's SQLite no-cap idiom) are SQLite-specific. PG needs `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` for the claim and rejects `LIMIT -1` (must be `OFFSET n` bare or `LIMIT ALL`). The async flip does not fix dialect; the pg adapter or the query layer must special-case these. Flag as separate from the async work but blocking real PG parity.

ON CONFLICT / upsert: cache sql-store uses `ON CONFLICT(key) DO UPDATE SET ... excluded.x` — PG supports this syntax, but `excluded` is portable; verify under live PG. content-store/queue DDL uses AUTOINCREMENT (SQLite) which PG rejects (needs SERIAL/GENERATED). DDL portability is out of scope for the async flip but will surface the moment a migration runs on PG.

Avoid any sync-over-async escape hatch (e.g. deasync, Atomics.wait). ADR 0006 explicitly forbids it; a single such shim re-introduces event-loop blocking and defeats the entire migration. Reviewers must reject any `prepare().getSync()`-style backdoor.

drafts.angle: CLEAN-CUT: make the SqlDatabase/SqlStatement seam fully Promise-returning with NO sync escape hatch, normalize the entire codebase onto ONE positional-array binding convention, and ship a first-class @keel/pg Pool adapter with real transactions. Accept a wider ripple (every consumer + createApp go async) in exchange for a single coherent async substrate and a driver-parity test architecture, rather than a sync-over-async shim that re-imports the blocking footgun the whole effort exists to remove (ADR 0006 step 1.1 explicitly forbids that shim). The cut is widened by one in-scope cleanup the prompt's facts did not flag: @keel/queue is the lone consumer using NAMED placeholders (`@queue`, `@now`) bound via Record<string,unknown> (packages/queue/src/types.ts lines 76-85, queue.ts enqueue/claim/reclaim/complete/fail/stats/find) — every other consumer (db, cache, content-store, migrate, workflows) is already positional `?`/array. A Postgres pg.Pool only binds `$n` positionally, so a clean async cut converts queue to positional `?` + arrays in the same pass; otherwise the pg adapter must carry a second named-param translator forever. This is the clean-cut differentiator.
drafts.pgAdapterPlan:
New package packages/pg (`@keel/pg`), peer-depending on `pg` (node-postgres) — a sibling to @keel/runtime's openSqlite, NOT inside @keel/db (keeps @keel/db dependency-free of any driver; matches how openSqlite lives in @keel/runtime). Exports `openPostgres(config): Promise<{ db: SqlDatabase; close: () => Promise<void> }>` returning the same shape as OpenSqlite.

Implementation:
- Holds a `pg.Pool`. The SqlDatabase wrapper:
  * exec(sql): `await pool.query(sql)` (multi-statement DDL; pg runs it in one round-trip via simple query protocol — do NOT parameterize exec).
  * prepare(sql): LAZY. Returns a statement object that, on first run/get/all, translates `?`→`$1..$n` ONCE and caches the translated text + a stable name (so pg can use server-side prepared statements via the `name` field). Translation: a single left-to-right scan replacing each `?` with `$<n++>`, skipping `?` inside single-quoted string literals and `--`/`/* */` comments (the only `?` in our SQL are placeholders, but the scanner is defensive). Because @keel/db owns every identifier and only values ride `?` (queries.ts header lines 17-19), the translator's input domain is small and fully enumerable in tests.
  * run(params): `const r = await pool.query({ text, values: params, name }); return { changes: r.rowCount ?? 0, lastInsertRowid: <see below> };`
  * get(params): `(await pool.query({ text: text + ' LIMIT 1'? no — @keel/db already adds LIMIT 1 }, ...)).rows[0]` → return rows[0] (undefined when empty; matches the `row == null` miss check in queries.ts:174).
  * all(params): `(await pool.query(...)).rows`.
  * transaction(fn): checkout ONE client from the pool (`pool.connect()`), `await client.query('BEGIN')`, build a tx-scoped SqlDatabase bound to that single client (so every query inside the fn runs on the same connection), COMMIT/ROLLBACK, `client.release()` in finally. This is the real-pool-correct transaction (the SQLite version is the degenerate single-conn case).
- lastInsertRowid on pg: Postgres has no implicit rowid. The queue is the only consumer reading it (queue.ts:200 enqueue → `Number(result.lastInsertRowid)`). Clean-cut resolution: the pg adapter detects a trailing `RETURNING id` is absent and CANNOT synthesize an id — so the QUEUE migration/insert must append `RETURNING id` and the adapter reads `rows[0].id` into lastInsertRowid. Documented as: "run() exposes lastInsertRowid only when the statement RETURNs an `id` column; SQLite supplies it natively, Postgres requires RETURNING id." This is a one-line change to queue.enqueue's INSERT (add `RETURNING id`) done in the queue-conversion step.
- Row mapping: pg returns snake_case column keys (our schemas already use snake_case column names — user.ts `password_hash`, queue `max_attempts`), and @keel/db's hydrate() (queries.ts:43-53) already maps snake→camel via table.byColumn. Raw consumers (queue.hydrate, cache CacheRow, content-store ContentRow) read snake_case keys directly, which pg preserves. Type coercion: pg returns BIGINT/NUMERIC as strings and BOOLEAN as JS boolean; our integer PKs are INT4 (returned as number) so fine; the `count()` path already does `Number(row.c)` (queries.ts:198) so a string count is coerced. Register a `pg` type parser for INT8→Number in openPostgres if any BIGINT columns appear (none today; note as a guard).
- Placeholder translation is the SINGLE Postgres-specific concern the prompt flagged (`?`→`$n`); it lives in exactly one function in @keel/pg and is unit-tested exhaustively.
drafts.createAppAsyncHandling:
createApp (packages/kernel/src/kernel.ts:92-112) must become async because migrations become async. Concretely:

  export async function createApp(config: AppConfig): Promise<App> {
    const migrationsApplied = config.migrations === undefined
      ? []
      : await new Migrator(config.db, config.migrations).migrate();   // migrate() now returns Promise<string[]>
    const application = new Application({ router: config.router, controllers: config.controllers, ...(config.middleware && { middleware: config.middleware }) });
    return { migrationsApplied, handle: (m, p, o) => application.handle(m, p, o) };
  }

KernelDatabase (kernel.ts:38-46) is updated to the same async signature as SqlDatabase (it is structurally the seam). The `App.handle` return type is already Promise<KeelResponse> — unchanged.

COORDINATION POINT for the router/web agent (do NOT design their side): createApp's signature changes from `App` to `Promise<App>`. They are rewriting createApp's relationship with Application/Controller into a code-first keel() router and changing AppConfig. The hand-off contract they must honor:
  (1) createApp (or whatever boot fn keel() exposes) MUST be `async` / return a Promise, because migration is now awaited. Whatever replaces `new Application({...})` must be constructed AFTER `await migrator.migrate()`.
  (2) Their handlers that today call `db.select()...get()` must `await` those terminals — the @keel/db terminal return types now carry Promise. This is a pure additive `await`; no shape change to what a query returns.
  (3) AppConfig.db stays the SqlDatabase seam (now async). If they reshape AppConfig, db must remain the async SqlDatabase.
This is documented in ADR 0006 as the kernel↔router seam; we implement createApp-async and they consume it.

CLI ripple (packages/cli/src/run.ts): every `const app = createApp(config)` / `createApp(config)` at lines 119, 137, 165, 219, 272, 302, 347 becomes `await createApp(config)`. These functions (runMigrate, runServe, runContentBuild, etc.) are ALREADY async, so the only change is adding `await`. runContentBuild also calls `persistEntries(config.db, entries)` (line 169) and `pruneEntries` (line 177) which become awaited.

Examples: examples/blog/serve.ts (insertPost line 42, countPosts line 44 → await), examples/blog/run.ts, examples/blog/src/app.ts (createApp line 24 → await; buildApp seeding), examples/estate/src/app.ts (line 44 `return createApp(...)` → `return await createApp(...)`, buildApp becomes async-returning). examples/estate/src/identity.ts threads the handle.

Pure helpers (hydrate/bind/render/compile/columns/conditions/table/DDL createTableSql) UNCHANGED. This is the root; everything else depends on it.

types.ts SqlDatabase/SqlStatement → async + transaction(). Migrator.migrate/rollback/status → async; ensureTable/appliedVersions await; REPLACE the manual exec('BEGIN')/COMMIT/ROLLBACK loop (migrator.ts:80-101) with `await this.db.transaction(async (tx) => { ...up(schema-over-tx); await insert.run(...) })` — the clean-cut win: transactions via the primitive, not string DDL. drafts.testMatrixPlan:
Two-axis strategy: (A) keep 100% coverage under the Node/vitest gate, (B) prove SQLite↔Postgres parity.

(A) Coverage under the gate (scripts/coverage-gate.ts runs each package's test:cov serially under Node):
- @keel/db, @keel/migrate, @keel/queue, @keel/cache, @keel/workflows, @keel/admin, @keel/content-store, @keel/identity: their unit suites already inject a FAKE SqlDatabase (in-memory). Flip the fakes to async (return resolved Promises) and `await` in assertions. 100% holds because the fakes execute every branch in-process — no driver needed. The transaction() branch (commit + rollback paths) is covered by a fake that throws to force ROLLBACK.
- @keel/pg: the placeholder translator, row-mapper, lastInsertRowid-from-RETURNING, and transaction commit/rollback are unit-tested against a FAKE pg.Pool (a stub exposing `query`/`connect`/`release`) — NO real Postgres needed for the coverage gate. This is essential: the gate runs under Node with no DB container, mirroring how runtime/sqlite.ts is covered with fake engines (sqlite.ts:19-23 doctrine). The real `pg` Pool construction (the untestable `new Pool()` wiring) is isolated into a tiny `pg-driver.ts` excluded from coverage exactly like sqlite-drivers.ts and bin.ts.
- @keel/runtime sqlite.ts: existing fake-engine tests, now async-asserted; transaction() commit/rollback branches covered with a fake `raw` that records BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK and one that throws.

(B) Parity proof (does NOT run under the 100%-gate; runs as its own CI step like @keel/integration/@keel/e2e which "declare no test:cov" per coverage-gate.ts:17):
- A new parity suite in @keel/integration (or a new @keel/db-parity package) defines ONE table of conformance tests against the SqlDatabase contract: insert→get, all+orderBy+limit+offset, count, update changes-count, delete, transaction commit-visible / rollback-invisible, ?→$n round-trip, RETURNING id → lastInsertRowid, snake→camel hydrate, null/boolean/undefined binding via bind().
- The SAME tests run twice via a parametrized `describe.each([openSqlite, openPostgres])`. SQLite leg runs under Node (better-sqlite3 loads). Postgres leg runs against a Postgres service container in the CI job (gated behind `process.env.KEEL_PG_URL`; skipped locally so the coverage gate is unaffected). This is PLAN.md step 1.8's "cross-driver test matrix" realized as a shared conformance contract — the clean-cut payoff: one test body, two drivers, proving the seam is honestly substitutable.
- Higher-level proof: run @keel/identity's existing register→verify→login integration journey and @keel/queue's claim/runOnce suite against BOTH drivers (per PLAN.md exit criteria "identity/queue/content all pass on Postgres and SQLite").
hazards: NAMED-PARAM divergence: @keel/queue is the ONLY consumer using `@name` placeholders bound via Record<string,unknown> (queue/types.ts:76-85; queue.ts enqueue/claim/reclaim/complete/fail/stats/find). pg.Pool only binds `$n` positionally. Must convert queue to positional `?`+arrays IN THIS CUT, or the pg adapter forever carries a second named-param translator. Risk: positional conversion of the multi-`@now`-reuse statements (complete/fail reuse @now twice) must repeat the value in the array — easy to miscount; covered by the queue unit suite.

lastInsertRowid is SQLite-native, absent in Postgres. Only queue.enqueue reads it (queue.ts:200). Must add `RETURNING id` to that INSERT and have the pg adapter read rows[0].id; SQLite ignores the extra RETURNING harmlessly (or the adapter special-cases). If missed, queue.enqueue returns NaN on pg.

exec('BEGIN'/'COMMIT'/'ROLLBACK') on a POOL races: consecutive exec calls may land on different pooled connections, so a BEGIN and its COMMIT hit different sessions → silent data corruption. The migrator's hand-rolled transaction (migrator.ts:80-101) MUST move to db.transaction() which pins one client. This is the single most dangerous correctness trap of the whole migration.

No-sync-escape-hatch is a hard rule (ADR 0006 step 1.1): a sync-over-async shim (deasync/Atomics.wait) re-imports the blocking footgun. Any reviewer 'just keep one sync path' request must be refused — it defeats the entire effort.

prepare() must stay SYNC or cache-at-construction breaks: cache/sql-store.ts (4 statements) and content-store/persist.ts cache prepared statements eagerly. If prepare() returned a Promise, those modules would need top-level await in factory functions (sqlStore is sync). Keep prepare() sync, terminals async; pg makes prepare lazy.

Migration.up(schema)/down(schema) callbacks: making Schema methods async means up/down become async-capable. Existing migrations (identity usersMigration up/down call schema.execute synchronously, user.ts:121-127) must be awaited by the migrator AND their callbacks may need `async`. If up() returns void and the migrator doesn't await schema.execute internally, DDL races the bookkeeping INSERT. Resolve: Schema.execute returns the exec Promise; up() either awaits or the migrator awaits the result — pick the explicit `up(schema): Promise<void>|void` and `await` it.

Coverage gate has NO Postgres container: the pg adapter's coverage MUST come from a fake Pool under Node, with real-Pool wiring quarantined in a coverage-excluded pg-driver.ts. If pg coverage depends on a live DB, the serial gate (which spawns `bun run test:cov` per package, no services) goes red. Mirror the sqlite-drivers.ts exclusion exactly.
bun:sqlite path never executes under the Node gate (sqlite.ts:16-17) — async wrapper logic must be proven entirely through fake engines; do not add a branch only the Bun runtime can reach.

CONCURRENCY shift: turning a sync data layer async changes interleaving. Code that relied on a query completing before the next synchronous statement (none should, but content-store persist loops + queue claim-then-update) now yields to the event loop between awaits. SQLite single-connection serializes anyway; on pg, two awaited statements outside a transaction may interleave with other requests. Anything requiring atomicity must use db.transaction(), not a sequence of awaits.

ROUTER agent collision: createApp signature change (App → Promise<App>) and the `await db...get()` ripple touch files the concurrent keel()-router rewrite also edits (kernel.ts, web). Coordinate via ADR 0006's documented seam; do not edit @keel/router or @keel/web — only declare the async contract they must consume.

content-store/migrate import SqlDatabase from @keel/migrate (not @keel/db) — two seam declarations (migrate/types.ts AND db/sql.ts AND kernel.ts AND cache/types.ts AND queue/types.ts) must be flipped in lockstep or structural typing silently diverges (one stays sync, a consumer compiles but a Promise leaks unawaited). Audit all 5 declarations together.

blockingIssues: MIGRATION up()/down() DDL-ORDERING TRAP (both drafts, incompletely handled). Real migrations write SYNCHRONOUS bodies that issue multiple sequential schema calls, e.g. packages/content-store/src/migration.ts up() does schema.createTable(...) then three schema.addIndex(...) with NO await; identity/user.ts, mailing-lists/models.ts, create-keel/templates.ts, examples/blog, examples/estate are the same shape. Once Schema methods (schema.ts createTable/addColumn/addIndex/dropTable/execute) await db.exec and return Promises, a synchronous up() that calls them WITHOUT await fires unawaited Promises. On SQLite the trivially-resolved wrapper preserves call order on the microtask queue so it passes (and the 100% gate stays green); on real Postgres CREATE INDEX can hit the wire before CREATE TABLE resolves -> 'relation does not exist'. Draft A NEVER makes up() async-capable and never awaits the schema calls inside up(): its DDL Promises are dropped and its bookkeeping insert.run() races the DDL. Draft B correctly NAMES this (interface up(schema):Promise<void>|void) but its checklist does NOT list rewriting the 6+ existing migration bodies to `async up`+`await schema.x()`, so its own resolution is incomplete: a sync up() body still drops the Promises even after the interface is widened. The only correct fixes are (a) rewrite every migration body to async/await each schema call, (b) have Schema buffer DDL and the migrator flush+await in order, or (c) collapse each up() to a single exec. Neither draft enumerates this blast radius.

ABORT/DEADLINE INTERPLAY UNADDRESSED (both drafts). runtime/src/server.ts withTimeout (line 348) races the handler against a deadline and explicitly ABANDONS the work promise to settle later; requestAbortSignal (line 727) publishes a per-request AbortSignal on client hangup. Today the SYNC data layer cannot be interrupted but also never yields mid-query. After the async flip, db terminals (.get/.all/.run/.count) and the pg pool query take NO AbortSignal and expose no cancellation. On deadline overrun or client hangup the request returns 503 but the in-flight pg query keeps holding its pooled connection until it naturally completes -> under load this exhausts pg.Pool. Neither draft threads context.signal into the seam (no `signal` param on terminals, no pool.query cancellation), so the async migration silently introduces a connection-exhaustion/cancellation gap that the SQLite dev path and the Node coverage gate cannot reveal.
missedConsumersOrCallSites: CLI FIRE-AND-FORGET createApp: packages/cli/src/run.ts:165 (runContentBuild) and :219 (runContentDelete) call `createApp(config)` and DISCARD the return — they exist only to run migrations before persistEntries/deleteEntry hit config.db. Once createApp is async, these become floating un-awaited Promises: migrations may not have completed (the schema_migrations / content tables may not exist) before persistEntries(config.db,...) at run.ts:169 / deleteEntry at the next line runs. Draft B's checklist lists run.ts line 165 but its prose only says 'becomes await' generically; NEITHER draft flags that 165/219 discard the result entirely, so a mechanical `const app = await` rewrite that pattern-matches on `const app =` will MISS these two bare-statement calls. Must become `await createApp(config)`.

createApp callers both drafts under-enumerate: packages/integration/test/{sites,http,identity}.integration.test.ts (3 files, lines ~79/109/187/266 — note http passes createApp(...) straight into serve()), packages/mcp/test/tools.test.ts:81, packages/kernel/test/kernel.test.ts (5 sites) and secure-stack.test.ts (9 sites), packages/cli/test/fixture/keel.app.ts, examples/blog/src/app.ts:24, examples/estate/src/app.ts:44 (`return createApp(...)`). Draft A lists 'mcp/integration/e2e harnesses' loosely; Draft B is more concrete but neither calls out that http.integration passes createApp result directly to serve() (serve must now await or accept a Promise<App>).

QUEUE NAMED-PARAM REALITY both drafts get the direction right but mis-state the current mechanism: queue is the ONLY named-param consumer (confirmed: grep for `.run({`/`.get({`/`.all({` outside queue hits only zod node_modules). Critically, queue's tests (packages/queue/test/queue.test.ts:21) cast better-sqlite3 Database DIRECTLY as SqlDatabase (`database as unknown as SqlDatabase`) and pass OBJECTS to .run/.get — so the declared `run(params?: unknown[])` type is ALREADY a lie that only works because (a) better-sqlite3 accepts a single object for @named binding and (b) queue NEVER goes through openSqlite's `...params` spread adapter. Draft A's claim that 'openSqlite's positional adapter cannot bind named objects via spread' is true but moot today because queue bypasses openSqlite; the real risk is the FAKE db in queue tests must be reshaped to async AND to positional arrays simultaneously, and any other code casting a raw engine as SqlDatabase (integration tests cast `database as unknown as SqlDatabase`) will silently keep compiling while leaking unawaited Promises.
sequencingHazards: Draft A claims the gate is green after EACH package commit ('never a flag-day'). This is FALSE for a structural-typing seam: the moment @keel/db's SqlStatement returns Promise but a consumer's local SqlDatabase declaration (migrate/types.ts, cache/types.ts, queue/types.ts, kernel KernelDatabase) is still sync, TypeScript structural typing lets the consumer compile against the OLD shape while a Promise leaks unawaited at runtime — tests that assert on a now-Promise value fail, so that package's gate goes RED until its own seam + awaits land. The five seam declarations (db/sql.ts, kernel.ts, migrate/types.ts, cache/types.ts, queue/types.ts) cannot be flipped one-package-per-commit and stay green; Draft B's 'flip all five in lockstep' (its checklist step 2) is correct and Draft A's strict-dependency-order-green-after-each is not achievable without also touching every dependent's awaits in the SAME commit.

createApp->async is a hard merge collision with the concurrent router/web rewrite (both drafts correctly fence this), but Draft A proposes landing 'db/migrate/kernel interface flip + the await in createApp together' while ALSO saying it does NOT touch the Application-construction block — yet createApp's body (kernel.ts:92-112) constructs `new Application({...})` inline. You cannot add `await new Migrator(...).migrate()` and change the return type without editing the exact function the router agent is rewriting. The only safe sequence is: land the seam flip + KernelDatabase first, and coordinate so ONE agent owns the createApp body edit; documenting 'await createApp' as a contract is necessary but not sufficient if both diffs touch lines 92-112.

Order dependency on migrate: making Schema async (the up()/down() trap above) must be resolved BEFORE or IN the same change as the migrator's transaction rewrite, because Draft B rebinds schema to the tx-scoped SqlDatabase inside db.transaction(fn). If up() is sync-void and drops Promises, the tx may COMMIT before the DDL Promises resolve on pg — the transaction wraps nothing.
correctnessRisks: TRANSACTION-ON-A-POOL is the sharpest trap and BOTH drafts identify it (Draft A hazard #5, Draft B hazard #3): migrator.ts:80-101 issues exec('BEGIN')/exec('COMMIT')/exec('ROLLBACK') as separate calls; on a pg.Pool each lands on a possibly-different pooled connection, so BEGIN/COMMIT do not share a session and the per-migration atomicity SILENTLY no-ops. SQLite (single connection) hides it; only live-PG catches it. Draft B's first-class db.transaction(fn) that checks out ONE client via pool.connect() and pins every query inside fn to that client is the CORRECT primitive; Draft A keeps exec('BEGIN') string DDL and only mentions acquiring a dedicated connection in prose without adding a transaction() seam — Draft A's approach is materially weaker here and is itself a latent corruption bug.

lastInsertRowid: Postgres has NO implicit rowid; queue.ts:200 enqueue returns Number(result.lastInsertRowid). Both drafts correctly require enqueue -> `INSERT ... RETURNING id`.get(). @keel/db's plain insert.run() (queries.ts:277) returns only {changes} so it is safe; .returning().get() emits RETURNING * (queries.ts:264) which pg honors. Draft B's framing ('run() exposes lastInsertRowid only when the statement RETURNs an id column') is cleaner. NOTE neither draft addresses that SqlStatement.run's TYPE still requires `lastInsertRowid: number|bigint` (non-optional) — the pg adapter must synthesize a value (NaN/0) for non-RETURNING inserts or the type breaks; better to make it optional, which is an un-flagged interface detail.

?->$n TRANSLATION + dialect drift: both flag ?->$n. Beyond placeholders the SQLite-specific idioms WILL break on pg and neither draft fixes them in the async work (correctly scoped as separate but both note as blocking real parity): renderSelect's `LIMIT -1 OFFSET n` (queries.ts:140 — pg rejects LIMIT -1), queue's `UPDATE ... WHERE id=(SELECT ... LIMIT 1) RETURNING *` needs FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for real concurrency, queue/cache/content DDL uses `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` (queue.ts:37 — pg needs SERIAL/GENERATED), cache upsert `ON CONFLICT(key) DO UPDATE SET ... excluded.x` (sql-store.ts:41 — portable but unverified). Both list these; correct.

COVERAGE-GATE REACHABILITY: claim is HONEST for the SQLite+fakes path — the async flip does not change which branches exist (get() row==null vs row at queries.ts:174 still both hit behind await), and existing fakes/better-sqlite3 execute every line under Node. The bun:sqlite branch (sqlite.ts:70) still never runs under the Node gate and is covered via injected fake engines (runtime/test/sqlite.test.ts:58 already does betterSqlite:()=>undefined to force fallback) — async-wrapping sync fakes keeps that branch covered. @keel/pg's 100% MUST come from a fake Pool with real `new Pool()` quarantined in a coverage-excluded driver file (Draft B's pg-driver.ts mirror of sqlite-drivers.ts) — Draft B states this; Draft A says 'fake async pg client' but does NOT quarantine the real Pool construction into an excluded file, so Draft A's @keel/pg risks an uncoverable `new Pool()` line failing the gate. The transaction() commit AND rollback branches need a throwing fake — migrate already has a throwing-migration test (migrate test line 327/334) proving the rollback arm is reachable; cache/content prepare-once-loop (persist.ts:52 prepares once then loops .run) confirms prepare() MUST stay sync (both drafts keep it sync — correct).

exec() return narrowing: confirmed no product code consumes exec()'s return (grep for `=...exec(` hits only regex .exec). Draft B narrowing exec(sql):Promise<void> is safe and slightly better than Draft A's Promise<unknown>. Minor.

CONCURRENCY/INTERLEAVING shift: Draft B's hazard #9 correctly notes that turning sync->async changes interleaving — content-store persist loop (persist.ts:56 for-of with sequential .run) and queue claim-then-update now yield to the event loop between awaits; on pg, two awaited statements OUTSIDE a transaction can interleave with other requests. Anything needing atomicity must use db.transaction(), not a sequence of awaits. Draft A does not raise this. The persist loop awaiting each statement.run sequentially is fine for correctness but is now N round-trips of real latency on pg (was N sync calls) — a perf regression neither quantifies.
critiques.strongerWhere: "Draft B is stronger on the two highest-severity correctness dimensions: (1) it adds a first-class db.transaction(fn) primitive that pins one pooled connection, which is the ONLY correct way to make migrator atomicity work on a pg.Pool — Draft A keeps raw exec('BEGIN') string DDL and only hand-waves 'acquire a dedicated connection', leaving a latent silent-corruption bug; (2) Draft B explicitly normalizes queue off named params to positional arrays in the same cut and quarantines `new Pool()` into a coverage-excluded pg-driver.ts (mirroring sqlite-drivers.ts), so its 100%-gate story for @keel/pg is actually reachable, whereas Draft A's pg unit-test plan does not isolate the uncoverable real-Pool construction. Draft B also flags the up()/down() async-capability problem (even if its checklist under-specifies the fix) and the concurrency-interleaving shift, both of which Draft A misses entirely. Draft A is stronger ONLY on incrementalism rhetoric ('green after each commit'), but that claim is actually FALSE for a structural-typing seam (a half-flipped seam compiles while leaking unawaited Promises), so its central selling point does not hold. Neither draft handles the abort/deadline -> pg-query-cancellation gap, and neither fully enumerates the migration-body rewrite blast radius implied by async Schema methods; Draft B is closer but incomplete. Net: adopt Draft B's seam (Promise terminals + sync prepare + first-class transaction()), but harden it with (a) an explicit decision on up()/down() — make Schema buffer-and-flush OR rewrite all 6+ migration bodies to async/await, (b) AbortSignal threading into the seam for cancellation, (c) making lastInsertRowid optional on the run() type, and (d) fixing the two fire-and-forget createApp call sites in cli/run.ts:165/219."

blockingIssues: FALSE 'green-gate-after-each-commit' premise (both drafts, draftA most explicitly). Verified: every consumer test boots a REAL better-sqlite3 (`new Database(':memory:')`) and either wraps it in the REAL `createDb` (identity test line 109, admin test line 75, mailing-lists test line 69, content-store) or calls the package's own real DB functions (cache `installCacheSchema`, workflows `installWorkflowSchema`, queue). NONE inject a fake async db today. Packages export `./src/index.ts` directly (no build step) and workspaces symlink to source, and `test:cov` is `vitest run --coverage` with NO tsc. So a leaked unawaited Promise is NOT a compile error — it is a runtime value-shape bug. The instant @keel/db's `.get()/.run()` become async, `userRepo.findUserByEmail` (identity/src/user.ts:76) returns `Promise<User>`; identity's login/register tests assert on `user.email` and FAIL at runtime even though identity's source transpiles fine. Therefore @keel/db CANNOT land in its own green-gate commit ahead of identity/admin/mailing-lists. The minimal atomic unit that keeps the gate green is {db + identity + admin + mailing-lists + their tests} in ONE commit. DraftA's claim 'the gate stays green after EACH package's commit, never a flag-day' is materially false for the real-createDb consumers.

Queue is coupled to mailing-lists, widening the atomic flip. mailing-lists/test imports the REAL `Queue` and `installSchema` from @keel/queue (test line 7) AND the real `createDb`. So when queue's seam flips async, mailing-lists breaks too. Combined with the db coupling above, the true minimal green-preserving commit is {db, queue, identity, admin, mailing-lists}, not the granular per-package sequence either draft lays out. Neither draft identifies that mailing-lists is the join point coupling the db-flip and the queue-flip into one atomic unit.

Both drafts undercount the seam declarations. Verified 8 independent structural declarations, not 5: db/sql.ts, kernel.ts (KernelDatabase), migrate/types.ts, cache/types.ts, queue/types.ts, workflows/types.ts (MISSED by draftB), runtime/sqlite.ts (SqliteHandle — MISSED), and orm/types.ts (MISSED; @keel/orm is a gated package with test:cov but is imported by NO consumer — a dead parallel seam that still must stay green or be excluded). DraftB's hazard-11 'audit all 5 declarations together' is wrong on the count; the workflows and runtime seams are real and the orm seam is an unreferenced gated package that will silently red the gate if its own tests assert sync values after a careless global edit.
missedConsumersOrCallSites: @keel/orm (packages/orm) — gated (has test:cov) but imported by no consumer; both drafts omit it entirely from the ripple. It must either be flipped or have its test:cov excluded, or the gate reds on a package neither draft touches.

workflows/types.ts and runtime/sqlite.ts (SqliteHandle) are independent seam declarations both drafts under-counted (draftB says 5 seams; there are 8). The SqliteHandle stays sync per both plans, but it IS a separate declaration that must be reasoned about as a fourth shape (raw engine, sync) distinct from KernelDatabase (async wrapper).

Queue does NOT flow through openSqlite. Verified queue/test boots better-sqlite3 directly (cast to SqlDatabase) and queue is wired in examples/estate/src/identity.ts via a raw handle, because openSqlite's adapter does `statement.run(...params)` (sqlite.ts:79) which spreads — it CANNOT bind queue's NAMED-object params. DraftA states this caveat; draftB states it; but neither flags that the estate example wiring (identity.ts) hands queue a handle whose async-wrapped openSqlite form would BREAK queue's named binding even on SQLite if anyone naively routes queue through openSqlite during the migration. The positional conversion is therefore not optional cleanup — it is required the moment queue shares the openSqlite adapter.

CLI run.ts has 7 createApp call sites (lines 119,137,165,219,272,302,347); both drafts list them, but lines 165 and 219 call `createApp(config)` WITHOUT assigning the result (fire-and-forget for migrate/content paths). Once createApp is async, an un-awaited `createApp(config)` there silently swallows migration errors and races — a `void` call that now returns a floating Promise. Both drafts say 'add await' generically but neither calls out the discard-result sites as the dangerous ones.

content-store imports SqlDatabase from @keel/migrate (verified hydrate.ts/load.ts/write.ts line 2), NOT @keel/db. content-store/persist.ts eager-prepares a single statement at module function scope (persist.ts:52 inside persistEntries, not at construction) — so the 'eager prepare survives because prepare() stays sync' argument (draftB hazard 5) is moot for persist (it prepares per-call), but IS load-bearing for cache/sql-store.ts which prepares 4 statements at sqlStore() construction (lines 36-46). DraftB cites this correctly for cache; draftA does not address the eager-prepare-at-construction constraint at all.
sequencingHazards: THE UNAVOIDABLE ATOMIC FLIP: contrary to both drafts' incremental framing, {@keel/db + @keel/identity + @keel/admin + @keel/mailing-lists + @keel/queue} must flip in a SINGLE commit to keep the gate green, because (a) identity/admin/mailing-lists drive the real createDb so they break the instant db's terminals return Promises, (b) mailing-lists also drives the real Queue so it joins the queue flip, (c) vitest does no typecheck so the breakage is a silent runtime Promise-leak caught only by value assertions. The genuinely-independent (per-commit-able) packages are exactly the ones that own BOTH their seam declaration and their own sqlite wrapper and are not consumed via real createDb: @keel/migrate, @keel/cache, @keel/content-store, @keel/workflows, @keel/runtime(openSqlite), @keel/pg(new). So the realistic sequencing is: (1) migrate solo, (2) cache solo, (3) content-store solo, (4) workflows solo, (5) runtime solo, (6) the ATOMIC {db+identity+admin+mailing-lists+queue} commit, (7) kernel, (8) pg, (9) callers. Both drafts' fine-grained 11-13 step per-package order is not achievable green at step 'db alone'.

createApp-going-async is the WRONG coordination framing for the actual router rewrite. Verified the router agent's new packages/web/src/keel.ts: keel() is a PURE router builder — it takes NO db, NO migrations, NO AppConfig; its handlers close over db themselves. The agent is DELETING Application/Controller (application.ts diff removes HandleOptions, moves it to types.ts). createApp's entire body — `new Application({router, controllers, middleware})` plus `new Migrator(config.db,...).migrate()` — is exactly what that agent is rewriting. So 'this work owns only changing createApp's return type to Promise<App> and awaiting Migrator, leave the Application block to the router agent' (draftA) describes editing the SAME function the router agent is replacing. There is no clean ownership split of createApp; whoever lands second rewrites it. The honest coordination contract is narrower: this work owns (a) the KernelDatabase interface flip in kernel.ts (a type-only edit the router agent likely doesn't touch), and (b) the FACT that migrations are now awaited; the router agent owns wherever migration-run lands in the new boot path. createApp itself is a merge collision, not a divisible seam.

Migrator's per-migration transaction (migrator.ts:80-101) uses raw `this.db.exec('BEGIN'/'COMMIT'/'ROLLBACK')`. On a pg Pool these land on DIFFERENT pooled connections, so BEGIN and COMMIT never share a session → migrations silently run NON-transactionally on Postgres while SQLite (single connection) hides it. DraftB correctly elevates this to a first-class `transaction(fn)` seam primitive (the better design); draftA keeps raw exec('BEGIN') awaited and only documents the pool hazard — meaning draftA's migrator is CORRECT on SQLite and SILENTLY BROKEN on the very Postgres path the whole migration exists to enable. This is a correctness divergence, not a style choice: draftA's plan ships a migrator that no-ops transactions on PG.

Migration.up(schema)/down(schema) callbacks are `up(schema): void` (migrator.ts Migration interface, lines 7-9). If Schema's methods (createTable/execute) become async, the existing migration callbacks (e.g. identity usersMigration calls schema.execute synchronously) will return un-awaited Promises that the migrator must await, AND the Migration interface signature must widen to `up(schema): void | Promise<void>`. DraftB names this (hazard 6); draftA's migrate plan says 'Schema builder methods await db.exec' but does NOT address that up()/down() are caller-provided callbacks whose signature is a public interface break for everyone who has written a migration.
correctnessRisks: Transaction-on-Pool is the single most dangerous trap and the drafts diverge on it. DraftB's first-class `transaction<T>(fn)` added to the seam NOW (pinning one pool.connect() client for the BEGIN..COMMIT span) is the only correct PG design; draftA's 'keep raw exec(BEGIN) awaited' silently corrupts on PG. Verified the live bug surface: migrator.ts:81-94 issues BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK as three separate db.exec calls. On SQLite single-conn this is atomic; on a pg.Pool it is three independent checkouts. DraftA does not fix it; draftB does.

lastInsertRowid: verified queue.ts:200 `return Number(result.lastInsertRowid)` is the ONLY consumer reading it (db's insert.run returns only {changes}, queries.ts:279). Postgres has no implicit rowid, so enqueue returns NaN on PG unless the INSERT gains `RETURNING id` and the adapter maps rows[0].id. Both drafts get this right. Note the queue DDL also uses `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` (queue.ts:37) which PG rejects (needs SERIAL/GENERATED) — both drafts flag AUTOINCREMENT as out-of-scope-but-blocking; correct, but it means queue's PG path needs BOTH the RETURNING id change AND a DDL dialect change, so queue is not actually PG-portable at the end of either plan without follow-up.

Named-vs-positional param fork is real and verified: queue/types.ts:76-79 declares run/get/all over `Record<string,unknown>` (named), every other seam uses `unknown[]` (positional). queue.ts binds @queue/@name/@now/@id throughout. openSqlite's adapter (sqlite.ts:79) spreads `...params` — incompatible with named objects, which is WHY queue is wired to raw better-sqlite3 today, not openSqlite. Both drafts converge on converting queue to positional `?`+arrays. Hazard neither fully prices: complete()/fail() reuse @now and @id multiple times in one statement (queue.ts:362-365, 376-397); positional conversion must repeat the value at each position and renumber on PG ($n) — a reuse-position miscount that only a live multi-param round-trip test catches, and the gate (sqlite, no PG) will NOT catch a $-renumbering bug.

renderSelect emits `LIMIT -1 OFFSET n` (queries.ts:140) as the SQLite no-cap idiom; PG rejects `LIMIT -1` (needs `LIMIT ALL` or bare OFFSET). queue's claim uses `UPDATE ... WHERE id = (SELECT ... LIMIT 1) RETURNING *` (queue.ts:220-228) which needs `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` on PG for real concurrency. cache/sql-store.ts:41 uses `ON CONFLICT(key) DO UPDATE SET ... excluded.x` (portable to PG, but verify). These are dialect divergences the ASYNC flip does not address; both drafts correctly scope them out, but both then claim a 'cross-driver parity suite proves identity/queue/content pass on PG' — that suite will FAIL on these dialect issues, so the parity step is gated on unscoped dialect work neither plan sequences. The parity suite is aspirational until the dialect layer exists.

Coverage gameability under the new async fakes: both drafts propose flipping consumer test fakes to 'return resolved Promises' and unit-testing @keel/pg against a 'fake Pool'. Verified the gate is vitest-with-no-typecheck. A fake that returns Promise.resolve(value) will make `await fake.get()` work, but it does NOT exercise that the REAL adapter awaits correctly — coverage % can stay 100 while a real `?`->$n bug or a missing await in the pg adapter ships, because the fake never reproduces pg's actual row/rowCount shape. Both drafts acknowledge this needs the separate live-PG step; the risk is that the live-PG step is non-gated (correctly, to avoid container flake) so a red parity suite does NOT block merge unless CI is configured to require it. Neither draft specifies the parity job as REQUIRED.
critiques.strongerWhere: "DraftB is materially stronger on CORRECTNESS and is the design to build on. Its first-class `transaction<T>(fn)` seam primitive is the only one of the two that produces a migrator that is correct on a Postgres Pool — draftA's 'await the raw exec(BEGIN)' approach ships a silently non-transactional migrator on PG, the exact target engine. DraftB also correctly forces the queue named->positional normalization as in-scope (not optional), names the Migration.up/down callback signature break, and isolates real-Pool construction into a coverage-excluded pg-driver.ts mirroring the existing sqlite-drivers.ts exclusion (verified that exclusion pattern is real). DraftA is stronger on COORDINATION FRAMING DISCIPLINE — it is more careful to not over-reach into router/web — but its central coordination claim (split createApp ownership: 'this work changes only the return type, router agent owns the Application block') is unrealistic given the verified router rewrite DELETES that whole block; both drafts mis-frame createApp as a divisible seam when it is actually a merge-collision function. Both drafts share the FALSE premise that the gate stays green per-package-commit; in reality db+identity+admin+mailing-lists+queue is one atomic flip because every consumer test drives the real createDb/Queue and vitest does no typecheck. Recommendation: take draftB's interface (transaction primitive, single positional convention, 5-seam-flip widened to the verified 8 declarations incl. workflows/runtime/orm), but replace BOTH drafts' incremental sequencing with the corrected order: flip migrate/cache/content-store/workflows/runtime individually, then land {db+queue+identity+admin+mailing-lists} as one atomic commit, then kernel, then additive @keel/pg, then callers — and treat createApp as a coordinated single rewrite with the router agent, not a divided edit."

synthesis.adrMarkdown:
# ADR 0006 — An Asynchronous Data Layer

- Status: Proposed
- Date: 2026-06-10
- Supersedes: none
- Relates to: ADR 0004 (`@keel/db` query layer), ADR 0005 (validation at the boundary), `docs/readiness/PLAN.md` Phase 1, `docs/readiness/2026-06-10.json`
- Coordination: in-flight `keel()`-router rewrite of `@keel/router` + `@keel/web` (see "Kernel ↔ router coordination" below)

## Context

`@keel/db`'s driver seam is **synchronous**: `SqlStatement.run/get/all` return values and `SqlDatabase.exec` returns a value (`packages/db/src/sql.ts`). The same shape is re-declared, structurally, in **eight** independent places — `packages/db/src/sql.ts`, `packages/migrate/src/types.ts`, `packages/cache/src/types.ts`, `packages/queue/src/types.ts`, `packages/workflows/src/types.ts`, `packages/orm/src/types.ts`, `KernelDatabase` in `packages/kernel/src/kernel.ts`, and the raw `SqliteHandle` in `packages/runtime/src/sqlite.ts`.

A synchronous seam can only ever be backed by an in-process engine (better-sqlite3 / bun:sqlite). A networked Postgres pool — the #1 production-readiness blocker — speaks over a socket and is fundamentally asynchronous. There is **no honest way to back a synchronous surface with a network pool** short of a sync-over-async shim (`deasync`, `Atomics.wait`), which re-introduces event-loop blocking — the exact footgun this work exists to remove.

We must flip the I/O boundary to Promises and propagate `await` through every consumer, then add an additive Postgres adapter.

## Decision

### 1. The async seam (no sync escape hatch)

All four driver I/O verbs become Promise-returning; `prepare()` **stays synchronous** (it only builds a statement handle — binding + execution is what touches the wire). We add a **first-class `transaction()`** primitive to the seam now, rather than leaving transactions to raw `exec("BEGIN")` string DDL.

```ts
export interface SqlStatement {
  run(params?: unknown[]): Promise<{ changes: number; lastInsertRowid?: number | bigint }>;
  get(params?: unknown[]): Promise<unknown>;
  all(params?: unknown[]): Promise<unknown[]>;
}

export interface SqlDatabase {
  exec(sql: string): Promise<void>;                                // was `unknown`; no caller reads it
  prepare(sql: string): SqlStatement;                              // STAYS SYNC
  transaction<T>(fn: (tx: SqlDatabase) => Promise<T>): Promise<T>; // NEW — first-class
}
```

Decisions baked into the seam:

- **`prepare()` stays sync.** `packages/cache/src/sql-store.ts` caches four prepared statements at `sqlStore()` construction time; forcing `prepare()` async would require top-level `await` in a synchronous factory. Keep `prepare()` sync, terminals async. The Postgres adapter makes `prepare()` *lazy* (translate-and-cache on first terminal call).
- **`exec()` settles to `Promise<void>`** — verified no product code reads `exec()`'s return.
- **`lastInsertRowid` becomes OPTIONAL.** Postgres has no implicit row id. Making it required would force the PG adapter to synthesize `NaN`/`0` for plain inserts. The only consumer that reads it is `@keel/queue.enqueue` (`queue.ts:200`), which moves to `RETURNING id` (see §4).
- **`transaction(fn)` is added NOW, not deferred.** The migrator needs atomic per-migration BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK. On a `pg.Pool`, three separate `exec("BEGIN")`/`exec("COMMIT")`/`exec("ROLLBACK")` calls land on *different pooled connections* — so the transaction silently no-ops while SQLite (single connection) hides the bug. `transaction(fn)` pins one connection for the whole span. This is a correctness requirement, not a convenience.
- **No sync-over-async shim, ever.** Any `getSync()`-style backdoor must be rejected in review.

### 2. `@keel/db` terminals

The six terminals in `packages/db/src/queries.ts` become `async` and `await` the driver: `SelectQuery.get/all/count`, `InsertValues.run`, `InsertReturning.get`, `UpdateSet…run`, `DeleteBuilder…run`, plus `Db.exec`. The public `Db` type's terminals return Promises:

```ts
get(): Promise<InferRow<T> | undefined>;
all(): Promise<InferRow<T>[]>;
count(): Promise<number>;
run(): Promise<{ changes: number }>;       // insert/update/delete
returning().get(): Promise<InferRow<T>>;
exec(sql: string): Promise<void>;
```

Pure value code is **byte-for-byte unchanged**: `bind`, `hydrate`, `renderSelect`, `compileInsert`, `quoteIdentifier`, columns, conditions, table. Chain *modifiers* (`where`/`orderBy`/`limit`/`offset`, `.values`, `.set`, `.returning`) stay synchronous and return builders; only the terminal verbs `await`. `createDb(sql)` keeps its signature — it only wires closures.

### 3. SQLite driver plan (the dev default, stays zero-config)

The two SQLite engines are synchronous; they present the async interface by trivially wrapping each terminal in an already-resolved Promise. Adaptation stays in `packages/runtime/src/sqlite.ts` `openSqlite()`. `SqliteHandle` (the raw engine shape) and `sqlite-drivers.ts` (the coverage-excluded native loader) are **unchanged** — only the wrapper gains `async`:

```ts
const db: KernelDatabase = {
  exec: async (sql) => { raw.exec(sql); },
  prepare: (sql) => {
    const statement = raw.prepare(sql);                 // sync, once
    return {
      run: async (params = []) => statement.run(...params),
      get: async (params = []) => statement.get(...params),
      all: async (params = []) => statement.all(...params),
    };
  },
  transaction: async (fn) => {
    raw.exec("BEGIN");
    try { const out = await fn(db); raw.exec("COMMIT"); return out; }
    catch (e) { try { raw.exec("ROLLBACK"); } catch { /* preserve original */ } throw e; }
  },
};
```

`async`-wrapping a sync return yields a resolved Promise — zero added latency on the dev path. SQLite is single-connection, so `transaction()` over one handle is correct; nested/concurrent transactions are an explicit non-feature on SQLite (the migrator runs serially). The injected `SqliteEngines` fakes stay **sync**; the wrapper makes them async, so the better→bun fallback branch stays covered under Node without ever loading `bun:sqlite`.

### 4. Postgres adapter plan — new `@keel/pg`

A new, additive package `packages/pg` peer-depending on `pg` (node-postgres), a sibling to `openSqlite` (keeps `@keel/db` driver-free). Exports `openPostgres(config): Promise<{ db: SqlDatabase; close: () => Promise<void> }>`, mirroring `OpenSqlite`'s shape.

- **Placeholder translation (the one PG-specific concern the seam owns):** `@keel/db`/`@keel/migrate` emit SQLite-style positional `?`. `prepare(sql)` translates `?` → `$1..$n` in a single left-to-right pass, skipping `?` inside string literals/comments (defensive — Keel only ever interpolates owned identifiers; every value rides `?`). Memoized per prepared sql string. Lives in one function (`src/translate.ts`), unit-tested exhaustively.
- **Lazy prepare:** `prepare(sql)` returns a statement closure; first terminal call translates + caches text and a stable `name` for pg's server-side prepared-statement cache. `run` → `pool.query({ text, values, name })`; `get` → `rows[0] ?? undefined`; `all` → `rows`.
- **`lastInsertRowid` reconciliation:** the adapter exposes `lastInsertRowid` only when a statement `RETURNING id` produced a row (`rows[0].id`); otherwise `undefined`. SQLite supplies it natively; **Postgres requires `RETURNING id`**. `@keel/queue.enqueue` adds `RETURNING id` to its INSERT (§ consumer ripple). `@keel/db`'s plain `insert.run()` returns only `{ changes }` (safe), and `.returning().get()` already emits `RETURNING *` which PG honors natively.
- **`transaction(fn)`:** checks out ONE client via `pool.connect()`, `client.query("BEGIN")`, builds a tx-scoped `SqlDatabase` bound to that single client so every query in `fn` runs on the same connection, then COMMIT/ROLLBACK and `client.release()` in `finally`. This is the pool-correct transaction; the SQLite version is the degenerate single-connection case.
- **Row/type mapping:** pg returns snake_case keys matching our DDL, so `hydrate()` (snake→camel) works unchanged. `count()` already does `Number(row.c)`, coercing pg's string-typed `COUNT(*)`. Register an `int8`→Number type parser as a guard (no BIGINT columns today).
- **Real `new Pool()` wiring is quarantined** in a tiny `src/pg-driver.ts` excluded from coverage, mirroring the existing `sqlite-drivers.ts` exclusion — so `@keel/pg`'s 100% gate is reachable against a fake Pool with no live container.

### 5. Migrator + `createApp` async ripple

**`@keel/migrate`:** `migrate()/rollback()/status()` return Promises; `ensureTable`/`appliedVersions` await; `Schema` methods (`createTable`/`dropTable`/`addColumn`/`addIndex`/`execute`) become `async` (each is one `await this.db.exec(...)`). The hand-rolled `exec("BEGIN")…COMMIT/ROLLBACK` loop is **replaced** with `await this.db.transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`, building a `Schema` bound to `tx`.

**The `up()/down()` DDL-ordering trap (must be fixed here, not just named):** existing migration bodies are synchronous and issue *multiple sequential* `schema.*` calls with no `await` (e.g. `content-store/src/migration.ts` does `createTable` then three `addIndex`; `identity/user.ts`, `mailing-lists/models.ts` call `schema.execute` twice). Once `Schema` methods return Promises, a sync `up()` fires **unawaited** Promises — on SQLite the resolved-Promise microtask ordering hides it, but on Postgres `CREATE INDEX` can hit the wire before `CREATE TABLE` resolves → "relation does not exist", and the bookkeeping INSERT can race the DDL. Resolution (both halves required):

1. Widen the callback signature: `Migration.up(schema): void | Promise<void>` and `down?(schema): void | Promise<void>`; the migrator `await`s the result.
2. **Rewrite every existing migration body** to `async up(schema)` + `await schema.x(...)` for each call (content-store, identity, mailing-lists, plus any in `create-keel` templates / examples / cli fixtures).

**`createApp` (`packages/kernel/src/kernel.ts`):** becomes `async` because migration is now awaited:

```ts
export async function createApp(config: AppConfig): Promise<App> {
  const migrationsApplied = config.migrations === undefined
    ? [] : await new Migrator(config.db, config.migrations).migrate();
  // ...construct the web core AFTER migrations resolve...
}
```

`KernelDatabase` is updated to the async seam. `App.handle` is already `Promise<KeelResponse>` — unchanged.

### Kernel ↔ router coordination (do NOT design their side)

A concurrent agent is rewriting `@keel/router` + `@keel/web` into a code-first `keel()` router that **deletes `Application`/`Controller` and reshapes `AppConfig`**. `createApp`'s body (`new Application({...})` + the `Migrator` call) is *exactly* the function they are replacing — so this is a **merge-collision function, not a divisible seam**. The honest contract this ADR fixes (everything else is theirs):

1. **Migrations are now awaited.** Whatever boot path replaces `createApp` MUST be `async`/return a Promise and must construct the web core *after* `await migrator.migrate()`.
2. **The db seam is async.** `AppConfig.db` stays the `SqlDatabase` seam (now Promise-returning). Handlers that call `db…get()/.all()/.run()/.count()` must add `await` — a pure additive `await`, no shape change to what a query returns.
3. **Identity methods are now async** (see §6): handlers calling `login`/`currentUser`/`verifyEmail` must `await`.
4. **Ownership:** whoever lands second owns the final `createApp`/boot rewrite. This work lands the type-only `KernelDatabase` flip and the await-migrate body; if the router rewrite lands first, this work rebases its await-migrate onto their boot fn. Do not race two diffs on the same lines — sequence the merge.

### 6. Consumer ripple — and where the gate CANNOT stay green incrementally

Critical, verified reality: consumer test suites boot a **real `better-sqlite3`** and drive either the real `createDb` (`identity`, `admin`, `mailing-lists`) or the package's own real DB functions / a raw engine cast as `SqlDatabase` (`queue`, `cache`, `workflows`, `content-store`). **`test:cov` is `vitest run --coverage` with NO `tsc`**, and workspaces symlink to `./src` (no build). Therefore a half-flipped seam does **not** produce a compile error — it leaks an unawaited Promise that fails value assertions at runtime. Draft A's "green after each commit" is **false** for these consumers.

The minimal green-preserving units are:

- **Independently flippable (own their seam + own sqlite wrapper, not driven via real `createDb`/`Queue`):** `@keel/migrate`, `@keel/cache`, `@keel/content-store`, `@keel/workflows`, `@keel/runtime`(openSqlite), and the new `@keel/pg`. Each can land green on its own commit.
- **ONE atomic commit — `{@keel/db + @keel/queue + @keel/identity + @keel/admin + @keel/mailing-lists}` + their tests** — because: `identity`/`admin`/`mailing-lists` drive the real `createDb` and break the instant db terminals return Promises; `mailing-lists` *also* drives the real `Queue` (`installSchema` + `Queue`), coupling the queue flip into the same unit. No `tsc` means the breakage is a silent runtime Promise-leak, caught only by value assertions — so these cannot be split.
- **`@keel/identity` public interface break:** `verifyEmail`, `login`, `currentUser` are **synchronous** today (interface lines 210–215) and call repo helpers that become async — they MUST become async (`verifyEmail(): Promise<User>`, `login(): Promise<{user,session}>`, `currentUser(): Promise<User|undefined>`). This is the cross-agent signal in §5(3).
- **`@keel/queue` named→positional normalization (in scope, not optional):** queue is the ONLY consumer using named params (`@queue`, `@now`, …) over `Record<string,unknown>` (`queue/types.ts:76`), which is why it is wired to a raw engine, not `openSqlite` (whose `...params` spread cannot bind named objects). pg binds `$n` positionally. Convert queue to positional `?` + arrays, repeating reused values at each position, and add `RETURNING id` to `enqueue`.
- **`@keel/orm` is a dead but gated seam:** it has `test:cov` but is imported by no consumer. A careless global seam edit will red its gate. Either flip its `types.ts` to async in lockstep, or leave it untouched (it is self-contained) — but it must be consciously handled, not forgotten.

### 7. Test matrix (keep the gate green; prove parity separately)

- **Coverage gate (`scripts/coverage-gate.ts`, serial, Node/vitest, no container):** unchanged in scope. The async flip does not change which branches exist — `get()`'s `row == null` hit/miss still both execute behind `await`. Each suite's existing real-better-sqlite3 / fake engine executes every line; assertions add `await`. The `bun:sqlite` branch still never runs under Node and stays covered via sync `SqliteEngines` fakes. `transaction()` commit + rollback branches are covered with a fake that throws to force ROLLBACK. `@keel/pg` reaches 100% against a **fake Pool** (stub `query`/`connect`/`release`); real `new Pool()` is in the coverage-excluded `pg-driver.ts`.
- **Parity (NON-gated, its own CI step, like `@keel/integration`/`@keel/e2e`):** a cross-driver conformance suite — ONE test body, `describe.each([openSqlite, openPostgres])` — covering insert→get, all+orderBy+limit+offset, count, update/delete change-counts, transaction commit-visible / rollback-invisible, `?`→`$n` round-trip with a reused-position param, `RETURNING id`→`lastInsertRowid`, snake→camel hydrate, null/boolean binding. Plus the identity register→verify→login and queue enqueue→claim→complete journeys on both engines. The Postgres leg is gated behind `KEEL_PG_URL` (skipped locally) so the coverage gate never depends on a container.

## Consequences

- A single, honest async substrate; Postgres becomes backable without a blocking shim.
- Wider ripple: every db consumer + `createApp` + every `createApp` caller goes async.
- New correctness surface (interleaving, cancellation) — see hazards/open questions.
- `@keel/pg` lands additively; the SQLite dev default is untouched and stays zero-config.
- Real PG portability is **not complete** at the end of this work: SQL dialect drift (`LIMIT -1`, `AUTOINCREMENT`, `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) is out of scope and tracked as follow-up — the parity suite will surface, not fix, these.

orderedChecklist.step: Write and commit ADR 0006 (this document): async seam with sync prepare + first-class transaction(); no sync escape hatch; lastInsertRowid optional; queue named→positional + RETURNING id; up()/down() widened to Promise-capable AND existing bodies rewritten; createApp→async as a router merge-collision coordination point; the atomic-flip sequencing.
orderedChecklist.acceptance: ADR committed listing all 8 seam declarations, the atomic {db+queue+identity+admin+mailing-lists} unit, the lastInsertRowid/RETURNING reconciliation, and the keel()-router contract.

orderedChecklist.step: Flip @keel/migrate: types.ts seam→async + add transaction(); Schema methods async; REPLACE exec('BEGIN')/COMMIT/ROLLBACK loop with db.transaction(async tx => …) building a tx-scoped Schema; widen Migration.up/down to void|Promise<void> and await them; migrate/rollback/status return Promises.
orderedChecklist.acceptance: @keel/migrate test:cov 100%; per-migration atomicity via db.transaction (not raw BEGIN); commit + rollback branches covered via a throwing-migration fake; migrate()/rollback() awaited in tests.

orderedChecklist.step: Flip @keel/runtime openSqlite: wrap raw sync exec/run/get/all in async; implement transaction() via raw BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK on the single connection. SqliteHandle + sqlite-drivers.ts UNCHANGED; injected fakes stay sync.
orderedChecklist.acceptance: runtime test:cov 100%; better→bun fallback branch covered with async-wrapped sync fakes; transaction commit/rollback branches covered; bun:sqlite never loaded under Node.

orderedChecklist.step: Flip @keel/cache: types.ts seam→async; sqlStore keeps eager prepare() at construction (prepare stays sync); get/set/delete/clear await terminals; installCacheSchema async; CacheStore interface + MemoryStore parity become Promise-returning; Cache facade awaits.
orderedChecklist.acceptance: @keel/cache test:cov 100% with awaited terminals; the 4 statements still cached at sqlStore() construction; MemoryStore async-shaped to match CacheStore.

orderedChecklist.step: Flip @keel/content-store: imports SqlDatabase from @keel/migrate (now async); loadEntries/loadEntry/createEntry/updateEntry/deleteEntry/persistEntries/pruneEntries/hydrateRuntime become async and await prepare().all()/.run(); prune/persist loops await each statement.run sequentially. crossCuttingHazards: THE GATE CANNOT STAY GREEN PER-PACKAGE for the createDb/Queue consumers. test:cov runs vitest with NO tsc, and identity/admin/mailing-lists drive the real createDb while mailing-lists also drives the real Queue. So {db+queue+identity+admin+mailing-lists} MUST flip in ONE commit; a partial flip leaks unawaited Promises that fail value assertions at runtime, not compile time. Draft A's 'green after each commit' is false.

TRANSACTION-ON-A-POOL silent no-op: migrator.ts:80-101 issues exec('BEGIN')/COMMIT/ROLLBACK as separate calls; on a pg.Pool each lands on a possibly-different pooled connection, so the transaction wraps nothing and SQLite hides it. MUST move to a first-class db.transaction(fn) that pins one pool.connect() client. Single most dangerous correctness trap.

up()/down() DDL-ORDERING TRAP: existing migration bodies are sync and call multiple schema.* methods with no await (content-store: createTable + 3 addIndex; identity/mailing-lists: 2x schema.execute). After Schema methods return Promises, a sync up() drops Promises — on PG, CREATE INDEX races CREATE TABLE. Fix requires BOTH widening Migration.up/down to void|Promise<void> AND rewriting every existing body to async/await.

lastInsertRowid is SQLite-only: queue.enqueue (queue.ts:200) is the sole reader. Make the field OPTIONAL on the run() type; queue moves to INSERT ... RETURNING id and the pg adapter reads rows[0].id. Otherwise enqueue returns NaN on PG.

NAMED→POSITIONAL fork: queue is the only named-param consumer (queue/types.ts:76, Record<string,unknown>), wired to a raw engine because openSqlite's ...params spread can't bind objects. pg binds $n positionally. Convert queue to positional ? + arrays; reused values (@now/@id appear twice in complete/fail) must be repeated at each position and renumbered for $n — a miscount only a live multi-param round-trip catches (the SQLite gate will NOT).

createApp is a MERGE-COLLISION function, not a divisible seam. The concurrent keel()-router rewrite deletes the very Application-construction block this work would edit. Coordinate so ONE agent owns the final createApp/boot rewrite; this work lands only the KernelDatabase type flip + await-migrate, rebasing if the router lands first. Both diffs touching kernel.ts:92-112 will collide.

@keel/orm is a DEAD but GATED seam (has test:cov, imported by no consumer) — both drafts forgot it. A careless global seam edit reds its gate. Plus workflows/types.ts and SqliteHandle are independent declarations: 8 seam shapes total, not 5.

ABORT/DEADLINE → CONNECTION-EXHAUSTION GAP: runtime/server.ts withTimeout abandons the work promise on deadline; requestAbortSignal fires on hangup. The async db terminals + pg.Pool query take NO AbortSignal, so on timeout/hangup the in-flight pg query keeps holding its pooled connection until it completes naturally — under load this exhausts the pool. The SQLite dev path and Node gate cannot reveal it. Threading context.signal into the seam is unscoped here — flag as follow-up.

CONCURRENCY/INTERLEAVING shift: sync→async means code that relied on a statement completing before the next now yields to the event loop between awaits. On PG, two awaited statements OUTSIDE a transaction can interleave with other requests. Anything needing atomicity (queue claim-then-update, persist loops) must use db.transaction(), not a sequence of awaits. Also: the persist loop is now N network round-trips on PG (was N sync calls) — a latency regression.

SQL DIALECT DRIFT beyond placeholders is OUT OF SCOPE but BLOCKS real PG parity: renderSelect's LIMIT -1 OFFSET n (queries.ts:140, PG rejects), queue's claim needs FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, queue/content DDL uses INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT (PG needs SERIAL/GENERATED), cache upsert ON CONFLICT ... excluded (verify on PG). The parity suite's claim that 'identity/queue/content pass on PG' is aspirational until a dialect layer exists.
NO SYNC ESCAPE HATCH: any deasync/Atomics.wait sync-over-async shim re-imports the blocking footgun and defeats the entire migration. Reviewers must reject any getSync()-style backdoor.

FAKE-FOR-COVERAGE BLIND SPOT: fakes returning Promise.resolve(value) make `await` work but do not reproduce pg's real row/rowCount shape — coverage can hit 100% while a real ?→$n or missing-await bug ships. This is exactly why the live-PG parity step exists and why it must be a REQUIRED CI status (see open questions).
openQuestionsForUser: AbortSignal / query cancellation: should this work thread context.signal into the SqlDatabase terminals (and pg's query cancellation) NOW to prevent pooled-connection exhaustion on deadline/hangup, or is that an accepted follow-up after the async flip lands? The gap is invisible on SQLite and under the Node gate, so it will not be caught until live PG under load.

node-postgres choice: is `pg` (node-postgres) the intended driver, or do you prefer `postgres` (porsager/postgres.js) which is async-native, faster, and has first-class tagged-template/prepared handling?
